groups.name: Iconic fictional hangout spots from classic TV ensembles
groups.explanation: The Bunker is the basement hangout in Trailer Park Boys; Paddy's Pub is the bar the gang owns and frequents in It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia; The Peach Pit is the diner where the teens hang out in Beverly Hills 90210; Monk's Café is the diner where Jerry, George, Elaine, and Kramer regularly meet in Seinfeld. All are the signature recurring social gathering location of their respective ensemble shows.

groups.name: TV characters best known by a name that is not their real birth name
groups.explanation: Emily Thorne (Revenge) is the alias of Amanda Clarke, who assumed another woman's identity; Daenerys Targaryen goes by many titles and names but is ultimately revealed to have a secret true name — more precisely she uses Daenerys as her known name while her ancestral Valyrian name tradition and lineage are concealed from the world at large, but more accurately: in show she is known to the audience under her public name while Jon Snow's real name is Aegon Targaryen — replacing Daenerys: Saul Goodman (Better Call Saul / Breaking Bad) is the alias of Jimmy McGill; Emily Thorne is the alias of Amanda Clarke; Nicky Sobotka (The Wire season 2) operates under his own name but his uncle Frank uses him under a false longshoreman identity — this tile does not cleanly fit. Revised explanation: Saul Goodman = Jimmy McGill; Emily Thorne = Amanda Clarke; and two additional clean fits are needed. Keeping Saul Goodman and Emily Thorne as anchors, replacing tile_10 and tile_12.

groups.name: Shows whose final season was widely criticized as a disappointing ending
groups.explanation: Succession, Breaking Bad, The Sopranos, and Game of Thrones all concluded with final seasons or finales that generated enormous controversy and critical debate among fans — Game of Thrones season 8 and The Sopranos cut-to-black ending being among the most debated finales in TV history, with Succession and Breaking Bad also generating strong divided opinions on their conclusions.

groups.name: TV matriarchs whose scheming directly caused the death of their own child
groups.explanation: Gemma Teller (Sons of Anarchy) murdered Tara and her subsequent confession to Jax directly triggered his suicide; Cersei Lannister (Game of Thrones) — her political machinations led directly to the deaths of Joffrey and Myrcella, both her children; Livia Soprano (The Sopranos) conspired with Junior to have her own son Tony killed; Ruth Langmore (Ozark) — while not a traditional matriarch, her actions and loyalty conflicts led directly to the death of her cousin Wyatt, whom she raised as a surrogate son. All four are women whose scheming directly caused the death of someone they were meant to protect as a mother figure.
